site.name: llm-xray
site.description: Rank the latest open-source LLMs by downloads and benchmarks, then x-ray any model's architecture layer by layer — attention, normalization, MLP, MoE — in interactive 2D and 3D.

home.heroSubtitle: Browse and rank the latest open models, then open any one to explore its architecture — every layer, attention pattern and formula — in 2D and 3D.

journey.title: From Zero to Hero: How an LLM Is Trained
journey.subtitle: Four stages turn random weights into a deployed assistant. Click through pretraining, fine-tuning, alignment, and evaluation — and watch its capability climb toward the leaderboard below.

diagram.sftResponseExample: Plants make their own food using sunlight…

loop.title: One training step
loop.intro: Every training stage is the same loop — only the data, the loss, and which weights move change.
loop.frozenStamp: weights frozen — forward pass only, just scored
loop.evalNote: Same machine, learning switched off: data goes in, a score comes out, nothing updates.

perStage.pretraining: Data = raw text · Loss = next-token · Adjusts = every weight
perStage.sft: Data = example answers · Loss = on the response · Adjusts = weights (or a LoRA adapter)
perStage.preference: Data = preferred answers · Loss = preference · Adjusts = the policy
perStage.eval: Data = test questions · nothing is adjusted

rlhf.title: RLHF — three steps
rlhf.beginner: Train a separate “reward model” from human comparisons, then push the assistant toward higher reward — kept on a leash so it doesn’t drift.
rlhf.step1: Humans pick the better of two answers (chosen ≻ rejected)
rlhf.step2: Train a Reward Model to score any answer
rlhf.step3: PPO nudges the policy toward reward — a KL leash keeps it close to the frozen SFT reference
rlhf.objective: Maximize reward, minus β times how far it drifts from the frozen reference

dpo.title: DPO — the shortcut
dpo.beginner: The shortcut: preference pairs update the assistant directly — no reward model, no RL loop.
dpo.noRewardModel: no reward model · no RL loop
dpo.caption: Preference pairs update the policy directly, still β-leashed to the frozen reference
dpo.notClosedForm: A reparameterization minimized by gradient descent — the closed form is the reward↔policy relation, not an analytic one-shot solution

lora.title: W = W₀ + (α/r)·B·A
lora.plainFull: Full fine-tune: every weight moves from the pretrained checkpoint
lora.plainLora: LoRA: freeze the base, train a tiny low-rank adapter (~1% of weights)
lora.mergeable: adapters merge back into W at inference — zero added latency

pretraining.title: Pretraining
pretraining.analogy: Like reading every book in a giant library and, on each page, guessing the next word before you see it.
pretraining.beginner: The model starts as random numbers and plays this next-word game across trillions of words. By the end it has absorbed grammar, facts, and how ideas connect — but no one has shown it how to be helpful yet, so it just continues text instead of following instructions.
pretraining.expert: Self-supervised next-token prediction over trillions of tokens of raw web/text. All weights are initialized from scratch and trained by minimizing token-level cross-entropy (negative log-likelihood) of the true next token given every previous one — no labels, the corpus supervises itself. The result is the base/foundation model: a powerful predictor with broad world knowledge but no instruction-following or alignment.
pretraining.whatChanges: Every parameter is trainable and updated from random initialization — embeddings, all transformer blocks and the LM head light up at once. This is where most compute goes and where world knowledge is encoded.
pretraining.dataExample: Raw text, no labels: "The Eiffel Tower is located in the city of ___" → predict "Paris". The whole sequence is both input and target, shifted by one.
pretraining.extraExpertNote: Scaling laws (Chinchilla / compute-optimal) govern the parameters-vs-tokens trade-off: for a fixed compute budget, loss is minimized by training a proportionally smaller model on more tokens (≈20 tokens per parameter). This base checkpoint is the frozen starting point for SFT, which in turn feeds preference tuning.

sft.title: Supervised Fine-Tuning
sft.analogy: An apprenticeship with worked examples: show thousands of question-and-ideal-answer pairs and nudge the model to imitate the answers.
sft.beginner: We show the model example conversations — a question paired with a great answer — and train it to copy their style and format. The raw text predictor becomes an assistant that actually responds to what you ask.
sft.expert: Supervised fine-tuning on curated (instruction, response) demonstrations with the same cross-entropy loss, but masked to the response tokens only — the prompt is context, not a target. Either full fine-tuning (update all weights from the pretrained checkpoint) or parameter-efficient via LoRA/QLoRA: freeze the base W₀ and learn a low-rank update W = W₀ + (α/r)·B·A, training under ~1% of the parameters.
sft.whatChanges: Full fine-tuning updates all weights from the pretrained checkpoint. LoRA/QLoRA freezes the base W₀ and trains only the injected low-rank adapters A and B — a slim trainable stripe over a frozen base. Toggle Full FT ↔ LoRA to see which parameters move.
sft.dataExample: Labeled demonstration — prompt: "Explain photosynthesis to a 10-year-old." → response: "Plants make their own food using sunlight…". Loss is computed only on the response tokens; the prompt is masked out.
sft.extraExpertNote: LoRA/QLoRA: rank r (≈8–64) and scaling α set adapter capacity; only A ∈ ℝ^{r×d} and B ∈ ℝ^{d×r} train while W₀ stays frozen, so adapters are tiny, swappable, and mergeable back into W at inference (zero added latency). QLoRA keeps W₀ in 4-bit NF4 and trains the adapters in higher precision — full-quality fine-tuning on a single GPU.

preference.title: Preference Tuning
preference.analogy: A mentor saying "this answer is better": people compare two responses and the model learns to lean toward the preferred one.
preference.beginner: The assistant can answer — but which answer is best? We show people two responses, let them pick the one they prefer, and train the model toward that style: more helpful, honest, and safe. It learns taste and alignment — not just a correct answer, but the kind people actually prefer.
preference.expert: Align the SFT model to human preference pairs (chosen y_w ≻ rejected y_l). RLHF trains a separate reward model on the comparisons, then optimizes the policy with PPO under a KL penalty to the SFT reference. DPO skips the reward model and the RL loop, minimizing an equivalent preference loss — derived from a closed-form policy-reward relation — by gradient descent directly on the pairs, widening the implied reward margin between chosen and rejected, regularized toward the reference by β.
preference.whatChanges: Weights shift from the SFT checkpoint toward preferred behavior, while a KL/reference term tethers the policy to the frozen SFT model so it doesn't drift too far (preventing reward hacking and mode collapse). DPO updates the policy directly; RLHF updates it via a reward model + PPO.
preference.dataExample: Preference pair for "Is it safe to mix bleach and ammonia?" — chosen: a clear safety warning about the toxic gas; rejected: a casual, unsafe reply. A human labels chosen ≻ rejected, so the model learns the ranking, not one gold answer.
preference.lossLabel: DPO preference loss (or RLHF reward model + PPO-KL)
preference.extraExpertNote: RLHF maximizes 𝔼[r_φ(x,y)] − β·KL(π_θ ‖ π_ref) via PPO, needing a trained reward model and an online RL loop. DPO uses the closed-form optimal-policy relation to fold the reward into a simple classification loss over the pairs — no reward model, no sampling. β controls how hard the KL tether resists drift from the SFT reference; too low and the policy can over-optimize and degrade.

eval.title: Evaluation & Deploy
eval.analogy: The final exam, then graduation: test the finished model on standardized exams, then shrink and package it to run fast for real users.
eval.beginner: We test the finished model on standardized exams — math, knowledge, coding, and safety — to see how good it really is, then compress it so it runs fast and cheap. The scores it earns are exactly the ones you'll compare on the leaderboard just below.
eval.expert: No weights train here — they're frozen and measured. Evaluate the aligned model on held-out benchmarks (MMLU, GSM8K, HumanEval, IFEval, GPQA, TruthfulQA) plus safety/red-team suites; the aggregate summarizes capability. Then post-training optimization — quantization (INT8/INT4) and an inference runtime — trades a small accuracy delta for large throughput and memory wins before serving.
eval.whatChanges: Nothing trains — all weights are frozen. Evaluation only measures them; quantization re-encodes the existing weights to lower precision (e.g. fp16 → int4) to shrink and speed up the model for serving, with no further gradient updates.
eval.dataExample: Held-out test item (GSM8K): "Natalia sold clips to 48 friends in April and half as many in May. How many did she sell altogether?" → graded against the gold answer 72. Accuracy is exact-match, averaged over thousands of items.
eval.lossLabel: No training loss — benchmark accuracy / pass@k
eval.extraExpertNote: Quantization for deployment: PTQ schemes (GPTQ, AWQ) and formats (INT8, INT4, GGUF, NF4) round trained weights to lower precision post-hoc — no gradients — typically costing a fraction of a point of accuracy for ≈2–4× memory and latency wins. pass@k (HumanEval) samples k completions and counts a problem solved if any passes the tests. These are the exact metrics plotted in the ranking below.

inference.title: How an LLM Answers: The Inference Journey
inference.subtitle: Watch a finished model turn your prompt into an answer — one token at a time.
inference.bridgeFromTraining: Training is done. Now watch that model actually answer.
inference.kvCacheNote: Seen tokens are remembered, not recomputed.

attention.caption: Each word looks back at earlier words and weighs which matter.

loop.caption: Append the new token and do it all again — until a stop token.

tokenize.title: Tokenize
tokenize.beginner: Your text is chopped into tokens — whole words or word-pieces — and each gets a number. Models don't read letters; they read these token ids.
tokenize.expert: A BPE/SentencePiece tokenizer maps the string to integer ids x ∈ ℤⁿ. Frequent pieces become a single token; rare words split into subwords, so any text is representable.

embed.title: Embed
embed.beginner: Each token id is looked up as a list of numbers (a vector), and its position in the sentence is mixed in so word order matters.
embed.expert: Each id indexes the embedding matrix E[tᵢ] ∈ ℝ^d; position is injected by rotating Q/K with RoPE. The result is an [n × d] matrix — the start of the residual stream.

layers.title: Decoder layers ×N
layers.beginner: Stacked layers refine the vectors. In each layer every token looks back at earlier tokens (attention), then 'thinks' through a small network (MLP). A residual stream carries information straight through.
layers.expert: Per layer: causal self-attention softmax(QKᵀ/√dₖ + M)·V over the cached keys/values, then an MLP (or MoE), each added back to the residual with pre-norm. The KV cache grows each step so earlier tokens aren't recomputed.

logits.title: Logits → Softmax
logits.beginner: After a final normalize, the model gives every possible next token a score, then softmax turns those scores into percentages — one probability per candidate.
logits.expert: The last token's hidden state is unembedded: z = W_U·norm(h_n) ∈ ℝ^{|V|}; then p = softmax(z) is the next-token distribution over the whole vocabulary.

sampling.title: Sampling
sampling.beginner: From those percentages, choose one token. Temperature, top-k and top-p control how safe or adventurous the pick is — greedy always takes the top one.
sampling.expert: Temperature scales the logits (z/T); top-k keeps the k highest, top-p the smallest set summing to p. Renormalize over the kept set and sample tₙ₊₁ ~ p.

loop.title: Repeat
loop.beginner: Add the chosen token to the text and do everything again to get the next one — appending word by word until a stop token or a length limit.
loop.expert: Autoregressive decoding: append tₙ₊₁, extend the KV cache by one position, and re-run the new token through the layers. Repeat until EOS or max length.

model.gatedNote: Requires access approval on Hugging Face
model.headGroupsHint: How query heads share key/value heads — fewer KV heads shrink the cache (GQA/MQA/MLA).

explorer.title: Architecture explorer
explorer.selectHint: Click any element to see its shape and what it does.
explorer.noWebgl: WebGL is not available in this browser — 3D view can't render.
explorer.drillHint: Click a layer to open its internal block.

ops.input_norm: Normalizes the residual stream before attention so values stay well-scaled.
ops.post_attn_norm: Normalizes again after attention, before the feed-forward network.
ops.q_proj: Projects the hidden state into Query vectors, one set per attention head.
ops.k_proj: Projects into Key vectors. With GQA/MQA there are fewer KV heads than query heads.
ops.v_proj: Projects into Value vectors — the content that attention mixes together.
ops.kv_a_proj: Compresses keys/values into a small latent vector (MLA), shrinking the KV cache.
ops.kv_b_proj: Expands the compressed latent back into full key/value space at use time.
ops.sdpa: Scaled dot-product attention: each token attends to others via softmax(QKᵀ/√d), then mixes Values.
ops.o_proj: Projects the attention output back to the model's hidden size and adds it to the residual.
ops.gate_proj: Gate projection of the gated MLP (SwiGLU/GeGLU): controls how much of each unit passes.
ops.up_proj: Expands the hidden state to the larger intermediate size.
ops.down_proj: Projects the intermediate activation back down to the hidden size.
ops.router: Scores experts per token and routes each token to its top-k experts.
ops.expert_gate: Gate projection inside one expert MLP (only the selected experts run).
ops.expert_up: Up projection inside one expert MLP.
ops.expert_down: Down projection inside one expert MLP.

compare.title: Compare models
compare.subtitle: Select models to compare across benchmarks and architecture.
compare.need2: Pick at least 2 models to compare.
compare.disclaimer: Benchmark scores are reported/approximate (public model cards & leaderboards); higher is better. Architecture values are exact, read from each model's config.

scatter.title: Capability vs. size
scatter.hint: Bubble = downloads · color = family · dashed line = efficient frontier (best score per parameter)

forward.sample: How far is the Sun from Earth?
forward.illustrative: Illustrative — shows the order of computation, not real model output.

learn.title: Learn
learn.subtitle: Understand how LLMs work — interactively, not from text walls.

softmax.title: Softmax & Temperature
softmax.blurb: How a model turns raw scores (logits) into next-token probabilities — and how temperature controls randomness. Drag the slider.
softmax.beginnerText: Higher temperature flattens the choices (more random, more creative). Lower temperature sharpens toward the single best token (more confident, more repetitive).
softmax.expertText: Each logit is divided by temperature T before exponentiation. As T→0 the distribution approaches argmax (greedy decoding); as T→∞ it approaches uniform.

attention.title: Attention
attention.blurb: Which earlier words does each word focus on? This is real attention from a small GPT model — drag the layer, switch heads, hover a word.
attention.beginnerText: Attention lets every word look back at earlier words and decide which ones matter for it. A bright cell means the row word is paying strong attention to the column word. GPT-style models are causal: a word can only look backward, so the top-right triangle is empty.
attention.expertText: Each head computes softmax(QKᵀ/√dₖ)·V over the causal-masked scores, giving a per-row probability distribution over keys. Different heads specialize — a previous-token head, an induction (copy) head, a syntactic head — and patterns sharpen across layers. Shown here: real distilgpt2 weights.
attention.head: Head
attention.flowHint: Tap or hover a word on the left to trace where it looks.

tokenization.title: Tokenization
tokenization.blurb: Models don't read letters — they read tokens. Type anything and watch it split into subword pieces, each with an id.
tokenization.beginnerText: Before a model sees your text, a tokenizer chops it into tokens: common words are one token, rare words split into pieces. Each token maps to a number (an id) the model actually works with. A leading space is part of the token (shown as ·).
tokenization.expertText: This is an illustrative byte-pair encoding (BPE): start from characters and greedily apply the highest-priority merge rules until none apply. Real tokenizers learn thousands of merges from data, use byte-level fallback for any character, and add special tokens like <bos>/<eos>. Vocabulary size trades sequence length against embedding-table size.

sampling.title: Sampling & Decoding
sampling.blurb: The model gives every token a probability — but which one does it actually pick? Tune temperature, top-k and top-p.
sampling.beginnerText: Greedy decoding always takes the single most likely token (safe but repetitive). Top-k keeps only the k best; top-p keeps the smallest set covering p of the probability; then the model samples one of them — here we highlight the most likely of the kept set. Temperature reshapes the odds: low = sharp and predictable, high = flat and creative.
sampling.expertText: Temperature scales logits before softmax (z/T). Top-k truncates to the k highest-probability tokens; top-p (nucleus) truncates to the smallest set whose cumulative probability ≥ p. After truncation the kept probabilities are renormalized and one token is sampled. These knobs trade coherence against diversity.

moe.title: Mixture of Experts
moe.blurb: Instead of one big network, MoE has many small expert networks — a router sends each token to just a few. Huge total capacity, a fraction of the compute per token.
moe.beginnerText: Every token is scored by a small 'router', which picks the top few experts to handle it. Only those experts run, so a model can have enormous total capacity while doing only a fraction of the work for each token. Each token's chosen experts are highlighted in amber.
moe.expertText: The router is a learned linear layer: g = top-k(softmax(x·W_r)). Only the selected experts' MLPs run and their outputs are combined by the gate weights. With E experts and top-k routing, each token uses k/E of the expert FLOPs — e.g. Mixtral's 8 experts, top-2 = 25%. Some models also add always-on shared experts.

rope.title: RoPE positions
rope.blurb: How does the model know word order? RoPE spins each token's vector by an angle that grows with its position — distance becomes rotation.
rope.beginnerText: Each pair of dimensions is rotated by an angle that depends on the token's position. Nearby positions get similar rotations; far-apart positions diverge. Low-frequency pairs barely move, high-frequency pairs spin fast — together they encode position. Drag the position slider.
rope.expertText: RoPE rotates the query/key dimension-pairs by m·θ_i where θ_i = base^(-2i/d). Because the rotation is relative, the dot product q_m·k_n depends only on m−n, so the model feels relative distance. A larger base (θ) turns the low frequencies more slowly, stretching the usable context length — the basis of NTK/YaRN scaling.

kvcache.title: KV cache
kvcache.blurb: To write each next word fast, the model remembers the keys & values it already computed — instead of recomputing attention over the whole prefix.
kvcache.beginnerText: When generating, the model would otherwise redo attention over every earlier token at each step. The KV cache stores those keys and values, so each new token only computes its own — much faster, but memory grows with length. Sliding-window models forget the oldest tokens to cap it. Press Generate.
kvcache.expertText: Each layer caches K,V for all past positions; a new token attends to the cache and appends its own K,V (O(seq) memory per layer). Total ≈ 2·L·n_kv·d_h·seq·bytes — GQA/MQA shrink it with fewer KV heads. Sliding-window attention bounds seq to the window, trading long-range recall for constant memory.

norm.title: Normalization
norm.blurb: Normalization keeps activations in a sane range so training stays stable. Compare RMSNorm and LayerNorm on a vector.
norm.beginnerText: Deep networks can blow up or vanish as numbers flow through many layers. A normalization step rescales each vector to a consistent size before the next block. RMSNorm just rescales; LayerNorm also re-centers around zero first. Randomize the input and compare.
norm.expertText: LayerNorm subtracts the mean then divides by the standard deviation, then applies γ,β: (x−μ)/√(σ²+ε)·γ+β. RMSNorm drops the mean term and divides by the root-mean-square: x/√(mean(x²)+ε)·γ — cheaper and empirically as effective, which is why most modern LLMs use it (pre-norm, before attention/MLP).

evolution.title: The Evolution of AI — From Perceptrons to LLMs
evolution.subtitle: Walk the arc that led to today's language models: classical machine learning, the first neural nets, the deep-learning and vision breakthroughs, sequence models, the transformer, and the LLM era. Click any era — or press play.
evolution.homeTeaserSubtitle: Seventy years of AI in eight steps — from hand-tuned algorithms to transformer-based language models.

classical_ml.title: Classical Machine Learning
classical_ml.beginner: Before neural nets took over, AI learned from tables of numbers that people prepared by hand. Algorithms like linear/logistic regression, decision trees, k-NN and SVMs found patterns in those features to classify or predict — powerful, but only as good as the features a human engineered.
classical_ml.expert: Statistical learning over fixed, hand-engineered feature vectors: linear and logistic regression, k-nearest neighbours, decision trees, and — pivotally — support vector machines with kernels for non-linear boundaries. Optimisation is largely convex (closed-form or quadratic/gradient programming), so training is stable and well-understood, but representation power is bounded by the features you supply.
classical_ml.keyIdea: Fit a function to hand-made features — no representation learning yet.
classical_ml.breakthrough: SVMs + kernels (1990s) gave robust non-linear classifiers with strong theory.

milestones.origin: Statistical learning takes shape — fitting simple models to hand-crafted features.
milestones.id3: Quinlan's ID3 popularises decision trees that split data by information gain.
milestones.svm: Cortes & Vapnik's support vector machine maximises the margin between classes.
milestones.rf: Breiman's random forests average many trees for robust, low-variance predictions.

architectures.linreg: A weighted sum of features squashed by a sigmoid into a probability.
architectures.dtree: A cascade of yes/no splits ending in a class vote at the leaves.
architectures.svm: Lift features with a kernel, then separate classes with the widest margin.

perceptron.title: Perceptron & Neural Nets
perceptron.tagline: Neurons that learn their own weights
perceptron.beginner: The perceptron (1958) was a simple model of a neuron: multiply inputs by weights, add them up, and fire. Stacked into layers and trained with backpropagation (1986), these networks could finally learn the features themselves instead of being handed them.
perceptron.expert: Rosenblatt's perceptron is a linear threshold unit; a single layer can't solve XOR (Minsky & Papert, 1969), which chilled the field. The thaw came with the multilayer perceptron trained by backpropagation — reverse-mode automatic differentiation of the loss via the chain rule — letting hidden layers learn distributed, non-linear representations end-to-end.
perceptron.keyIdea: Composable non-linear units trained by gradient via backprop.
perceptron.breakthrough: Backpropagation (Rumelhart, Hinton & Williams, 1986) made multi-layer nets trainable.

milestones.perceptron: Rosenblatt builds the perceptron — a trainable model of a single neuron.
milestones.minsky: Minsky & Papert show a single layer can't learn XOR, cooling the field for a decade.
milestones.backprop: Backpropagation trains multi-layer networks by sending the error gradient backward.

architectures.perceptron: One neuron: weigh the inputs, sum them, and fire past a threshold.
architectures.mlp: A hidden layer of neurons lets the network learn non-linear boundaries.
architectures.backprop_net: Stacked layers trained end-to-end as gradients flow back through each.

deep_learning.title: Deep Learning
deep_learning.beginner: Deeper networks — many layers stacked — learn far richer patterns, but they were hard to train until better initialisation, activation functions and, crucially, GPUs arrived. “Deep learning” is just neural nets with enough depth and compute to shine.
deep_learning.expert: Depth lets a network compose hierarchical features, but vanishing/exploding gradients made deep stacks brittle. Greedy layerwise pretraining (Hinton, 2006), ReLU activations, better initialisation (Xavier/He) and GPU-accelerated SGD on large datasets unlocked stable training of deep models — setting the stage for representation learning.
deep_learning.keyIdea: Add depth + GPUs so features are learned hierarchically, not designed.
deep_learning.breakthrough: GPU training + ReLU/better init made very deep networks trainable.

milestones.dbn: Hinton's deep belief nets show deep models can be pretrained layer by layer.
milestones.imagenet_data: ImageNet ships 14M labelled images — fuel for data-hungry deep models.
milestones.relu: ReLU activations keep gradients alive, making deep stacks far easier to train.
milestones.dropout: Dropout randomly mutes neurons during training, curbing overfitting.

architectures.deep_mlp: Many fully-connected layers with ReLU, trained by SGD on GPUs.
architectures.autoencoder: Compress the input to a small code, then reconstruct it — learning features unsupervised.
architectures.dbn: A stack of restricted Boltzmann machines pretrained greedily, then fine-tuned.

vision_cnn.title: Vision & Convolutional Nets
vision_cnn.beginner: Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) slide small filters over an image to detect edges, textures and shapes, building up to whole objects. In 2012 a CNN (AlexNet) crushed the ImageNet contest, and computer vision was never the same.
vision_cnn.expert: CNNs exploit translation-equivariance and weight sharing: stacked convolution + pooling layers learn a feature hierarchy from pixels. LeNet (1998) proved the idea; AlexNet (2012) scaled it on GPUs to win ImageNet by a wide margin; ResNet (2015) added residual connections, enabling 100+ layer networks. Depth and residuals later fed straight into transformers.
vision_cnn.keyIdea: Local filters + weight sharing learn a visual feature hierarchy.

milestones.lenet: LeCun's LeNet-5 reads handwritten digits with convolution and pooling.
milestones.alexnet: AlexNet wins ImageNet 2012 by a huge margin, igniting the deep-learning boom.
milestones.resnet: ResNet's skip connections train 100+ layer networks and surpass human ImageNet accuracy.
milestones.turing: Hinton, LeCun & Bengio win the Turing Award for deep learning.

architectures.lenet: The original CNN: two conv+pool stages feeding fully-connected digit classes.
architectures.alexnet: A deeper, GPU-trained CNN with ReLU and dropout that cracked ImageNet.
architectures.vgg: Depth from long stacks of small 3×3 convolutions — simple and uniform.
architectures.resnet: Residual blocks add a skip path so very deep networks stay trainable.

nlp_seq.title: Sequence Models for Language
nlp_seq.beginner: To handle language, models needed memory of what came before. word2vec turned words into vectors that capture meaning; RNNs and LSTMs read text one token at a time, carrying a hidden state; and “attention” let a model look back at the most relevant earlier words.
nlp_seq.expert: word2vec/GloVe gave dense distributional embeddings. Recurrent nets (RNN → LSTM/GRU) model sequences with a recurrent hidden state but struggle with long-range dependencies and can't parallelise over time. Sequence-to-sequence with attention (Bahdanau, 2014) let the decoder soft-align to encoder states — the direct conceptual ancestor of self-attention.
nlp_seq.keyIdea: Carry a hidden state over time; attend to the relevant past.
nlp_seq.breakthrough: Seq2seq + attention (2014) — the seed of the transformer.

milestones.lstm: Hochreiter & Schmidhuber's LSTM adds gates so RNNs remember long-range context.
milestones.word2vec: word2vec learns word vectors where meaning becomes geometry.
milestones.seq2seq: Sutskever's encoder–decoder maps one sequence to another for translation.
milestones.attention: Bahdanau attention lets the decoder focus on the most relevant input words.

architectures.word2vec: A shallow net that turns each word into a dense meaning vector.
architectures.lstm: A recurrent cell with gates that carries context across long sequences.
architectures.seq2seq: An encoder LSTM reads the input; a decoder writes the output, guided by attention.

transformers.title: The Transformer
transformers.beginner: The transformer dropped recurrence entirely and used only attention: every token can look at every other token at once. That made models hugely parallel (fast to train on GPUs) and great at long-range context — the architecture every modern LLM is built on.
transformers.expert: Self-attention computes softmax(QKᵀ/√d)·V so each position mixes information from all others in one step; multi-head attention runs several in parallel subspaces, and positional encodings restore order. Removing recurrence makes training fully parallel across the sequence and scales gracefully — the substrate the whole catalog you can x-ray here is built on.
transformers.keyIdea: Replace recurrence with parallel self-attention over the whole sequence.
transformers.breakthrough: “Attention Is All You Need” (Vaswani et al., 2017).

milestones.attention_paper: “Attention Is All You Need” drops recurrence for pure self-attention.
milestones.bert: BERT pretrains a bidirectional encoder and tops NLP benchmarks.
milestones.gpt: GPT pretrains a decoder to generate text, then fine-tunes per task.
milestones.gpt2: GPT-2 scales up and writes fluent paragraphs zero-shot.

architectures.transformer: Self-attention + feed-forward blocks, fully parallel over the sequence.
architectures.bert: An encoder-only transformer that reads text in both directions for understanding.
architectures.gpt: A decoder-only transformer that predicts the next token to generate text.

llms.title: Large Language Models
llms.beginner: Train a transformer to predict the next word on a huge slice of the internet, make it bigger, and surprising abilities emerge. BERT and GPT kicked it off; GPT-3 showed scale alone unlocks few-shot skills; then RLHF (InstructGPT/ChatGPT) taught models to follow instructions.
llms.expert: Self-supervised pretraining (masked LM for BERT, causal LM for GPT) plus empirical scaling laws — loss falls predictably as a power law in parameters, data and compute — drove a race to scale. GPT-3 demonstrated in-context/few-shot learning; instruction tuning and RLHF/DPO aligned raw predictors into helpful assistants. (See the Training journey on the home page.)
llms.keyIdea: Next-token pretraining + scale + alignment = a general assistant.

milestones.gpt3: GPT-3 reaches 175B parameters and learns tasks from a few examples in the prompt.
milestones.scaling: Scaling laws show loss falls predictably with more parameters, data and compute.
milestones.instructgpt: InstructGPT aligns the model to follow instructions via RLHF.
milestones.chatgpt: ChatGPT puts a conversational LLM in front of hundreds of millions of people.

architectures.gpt3: A very deep decoder-only stack — scale alone unlocks few-shot abilities.
architectures.t5: An encoder–decoder that casts every NLP task as text-to-text.
architectures.llama: A modern open decoder with RMSNorm, RoPE and SwiGLU — the template for today's LLMs.

frontier.title: Frontier & Open Models
frontier.beginner: Today's models see images and hear audio, route tokens to specialised “expert” sub-networks for efficiency, reason step by step, and increasingly ship as open weights anyone can run. This is the world the rest of llm-xray lets you explore.
frontier.expert: The frontier blends multimodality (vision/audio encoders projected into the text stream), sparse Mixture-of-Experts for parameter-efficient scaling, long-context attention variants and reasoning-tuned post-training. A vibrant open-weights ecosystem — Llama, Qwen, DeepSeek, Mistral, Gemma — makes these architectures inspectable, which is exactly what this site does.
frontier.keyIdea: Multimodal + sparse MoE + reasoning, increasingly open-weight.
frontier.breakthrough: Open-weight multimodal MoE models you can download and x-ray.

milestones.open_weights: Open-weight models — Llama, Mistral, Qwen — put frontier architectures in everyone's hands.
milestones.moe_mm: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts and native multimodality scale capability efficiently.
milestones.reasoning: Reasoning models are post-trained with RL to think step by step before answering.

architectures.mixtral: Each token is routed to a few expert FFNs — more capacity at fixed compute.
architectures.multimodal: Vision and audio encoders feed the text decoder, so one model sees, hears and reads.
architectures.reasoning: A decoder tuned by RL to produce long chains of thought before the final answer.

processes.title: Working with LLMs: Beyond Training
processes.subtitle: You have a trained, open model — now what? These are the processes teams run on an existing LLM to shrink it, adapt it, ground it, make it reason, serve it, and keep it safe.
processes.intro: Training and inference are above. This walks the practical lifecycle that turns a raw open model into a useful, deployed product.

quantization.title: Quantization
quantization.beginner: Store the model's numbers in fewer bits — 8-bit or 4-bit instead of 16/32 — so it takes far less memory and runs faster. Done well, quality barely drops.
quantization.expert: Map high-precision weights to a small integer grid with a per-channel or per-group scale (GPTQ, AWQ, GGUF). Post-training quantization needs no retraining; 4-bit roughly quarters the memory footprint, trading a small accuracy hit for big speed/VRAM wins — the difference between fitting on one GPU or not.
quantization.useCase: Run a big model on limited VRAM or consumer hardware.
quantization.tradeoff: Lower precision → smaller and faster, but a slight accuracy drop.

distillation.title: Distillation
distillation.beginner: Use a big, capable 'teacher' model to train a small 'student' that mimics it. The student is cheaper to run but keeps much of the teacher's skill.
distillation.expert: Train the student on the teacher's soft probability distribution (and/or hidden states), minimizing KL to the temperature-softened teacher logits alongside the task loss. The student learns the teacher's 'dark knowledge' — relative class probabilities — reaching quality a same-size model trained from labels alone can't.
distillation.useCase: Ship a fast, cheap model with near-teacher quality.
distillation.tradeoff: Smaller and cheaper, but capped by the teacher and needs its outputs.

adaptation.title: Adaptation
adaptation.beginner: Fine-tune an existing open model on your own domain or task — often with LoRA, which trains a tiny add-on instead of the whole model, so it's cheap and fast.
adaptation.expert: Continued pretraining or instruction/LoRA fine-tuning on domain data. LoRA freezes the base W₀ and learns a low-rank update B·A (~1% of params), merged at inference for zero added latency; QLoRA does it over a 4-bit base. (See the training journey above for the full picture.)
adaptation.useCase: Make a general model expert in your domain or style.
adaptation.tradeoff: Cheap with LoRA, but risks catastrophic forgetting or overfitting.

rag.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation
rag.beginner: Instead of relying only on what the model memorized, fetch relevant documents from your own data and put them in the prompt — so answers stay current and cite real sources.
rag.expert: Embed a corpus into a vector store; at query time retrieve top-k by similarity and condition generation on the retrieved context. Cuts hallucination and lets you update knowledge without retraining — quality hinges on the retriever and chunking, not just the LLM.
rag.useCase: Answer over private or fresh data with citations, no retraining.
rag.tradeoff: No retraining needed, but only as good as what's retrieved.

agents.title: Agents & Tool Use
agents.tagline: Let it act, not just talk
agents.beginner: Give the model tools — web search, a code runner, a calculator, APIs — and let it decide when to call them in a loop, so it can do real tasks instead of only chatting.
agents.expert: Expose tools via function-calling schemas; the model emits a structured call, the runtime executes it, and the result feeds back in an observe→think→act loop. Enables multi-step tasks and planning, but adds latency, cost, and failure modes (loops, bad calls) that need guardrails.
agents.useCase: Multi-step tasks: browse, run code, call APIs, automate.
agents.tradeoff: Powerful and autonomous, but slower, costlier, and harder to control.

reasoning.title: Reasoning
reasoning.beginner: Let the model 'think out loud' step by step before giving its final answer, or spend more compute trying several approaches. It's slower but much more accurate on hard problems.
reasoning.expert: Elicit chain-of-thought and scale test-time compute (sampling, self-consistency, search, or RL-trained long reasoning à la o1/R1). Accuracy on math and logic rises with thinking tokens — you trade latency and cost for correctness, marginalizing over latent reasoning paths z.
reasoning.useCase: Hard math, logic, coding, and multi-step problems.
reasoning.tradeoff: More accurate, but many more tokens → slower and pricier.

serving.title: Serving & Optimization
serving.beginner: Deploy the model so many users can hit it at once, cheaply and quickly — using engines that batch requests together and reuse computation.
serving.expert: Production inference engines (vLLM, TGI) use paged KV-cache, continuous batching, and speculative decoding (a small draft model proposes tokens a big model verifies) to raise throughput and cut latency. The art is maximizing tokens/sec/GPU under a latency budget.
serving.tradeoff: Big throughput gains, but real engineering and infra complexity.

safety.title: Safety & Guardrails
safety.beginner: Add filters around the model to block harmful inputs and outputs, and stress-test it ('red-teaming') to find weaknesses before users do.
safety.expert: Layer input/output moderation classifiers, system-prompt policies, and refusal training around the model; red-team for jailbreaks and adversarial prompts. A guardrail returns an answer only when the unsafe-probability is below a threshold — defense in depth, since no single layer is perfect.
safety.useCase: Production apps, regulated domains, public-facing assistants.
safety.tradeoff: Safer, but can add latency and sometimes over-refuse benign requests.

quiz.resultPerfect: Perfect score — you really know your LLMs!
quiz.resultGood: Nice — a solid grasp of the concepts.
quiz.resultTryAgain: Worth another pass — the section above explains it all.
quiz.shareText: I scored {score}/{total} on the “{section}” quiz at llm-xray {url}

training-pretrain-objective.prompt: What is the training objective during LLM pretraining?
options: Predicting the next token in text
training-pretrain-objective.explain: Pretraining is next-token prediction over huge text corpora — everything else emerges from that simple objective.

training-data-scale.prompt: Roughly how much text do frontier LLMs pretrain on?
training-data-scale.explain: Modern frontier models see on the order of 10 trillion+ tokens scraped and filtered from the web, code, and books.

training-sft-purpose.prompt: What does supervised fine-tuning (SFT) teach a pretrained model?
options: To follow instructions and answer in a helpful format
training-sft-purpose.explain: SFT shows the model curated prompt→response pairs so it behaves like an assistant rather than a raw text predictor.

training-rlhf-role.prompt: What role does RLHF / DPO play in training?
options: It adds new knowledge from the web
It aligns outputs with human preferences
training-rlhf-role.explain: Preference optimization tunes the model toward answers people rate as helpful and harmless — it adds alignment, not knowledge.

training-eval-benchmarks.prompt: Why are benchmarks like MMLU run after training?
options: To measure capabilities across many tasks
training-eval-benchmarks.explain: Benchmarks give a comparable score across knowledge, math, code and reasoning so models can be tracked and compared.

inference-first-step.prompt: What happens first when you send a prompt to an LLM?
options: The model writes a draft answer
The text is tokenized into IDs
The KV cache is cleared to zero
inference-first-step.explain: Everything starts with the tokenizer: text becomes a sequence of integer token IDs the model can embed.

options: Raw scores for every vocabulary token, before softmax
inference-logits-meaning.explain: The model's last layer outputs one raw score per vocabulary entry; softmax then turns them into probabilities.

inference-temperature-effect.prompt: What does raising the temperature do?
options: Flattens the distribution — more diverse, riskier picks
inference-temperature-effect.explain: Temperature divides the logits: higher values flatten the distribution, so sampling picks less likely tokens more often.

inference-kv-cache-why.prompt: Why do runtimes keep a KV cache?
options: To store chat history on disk
To quantize weights on the fly
To avoid recomputing attention over past tokens
inference-kv-cache-why.explain: Keys and values of every previous position are stored once, so each new token only attends — never recomputes.

options: When the GPU runs out of memory
At an end-of-sequence token or a length limit
inference-stop-condition.explain: The loop ends when the model emits its end-of-sequence token or the configured max length is reached.

processes-quantization-tradeoff.prompt: What is the core trade-off of quantization?
options: Less memory in exchange for a small quality loss
processes-quantization-tradeoff.explain: Storing weights in 8 or 4 bits shrinks memory ~2–4× versus FP16; aggressive levels start to cost noticeable quality.

options: Training a small student model to mimic a large teacher
Removing unsafe data from a corpus
processes-distillation-goal.explain: The student learns from the teacher's full output distribution, capturing more signal than raw labels alone.

processes-rag-benefit.prompt: What does RAG add to a plain LLM?
options: Fresh external knowledge retrieved at answer time
processes-rag-benefit.explain: Retrieval-augmented generation fetches relevant documents per query, so answers can cite data the model never trained on.

processes-lora-frozen.prompt: In LoRA fine-tuning, what happens to the base weights W₀?
options: They stay frozen; small adapter matrices learn the change
processes-lora-frozen.explain: LoRA adds a low-rank update B·A on top of frozen weights — training touches ~1% of the parameters.

processes-guardrails-where.prompt: Where do safety guardrails typically sit?
options: Around the model — checking inputs and outputs
processes-guardrails-where.explain: Production systems wrap the model with classifiers that screen the incoming prompt and the outgoing answer.

evolution-perceptron-year.explain: Frank Rosenblatt's perceptron (1958) was the first trainable artificial neuron — the ancestor of every deep net.

options: A GPU-trained CNN that crushed ImageNet
It was the first open-source LLM
evolution-alexnet-milestone.explain: AlexNet halved the ImageNet error using GPUs and deep convolutions, igniting the deep-learning era.

evolution-attention-paper.explain: Vaswani et al., 2017 replaced recurrence with self-attention — the architecture every modern LLM builds on.

evolution-rnn-limitation.prompt: What key limitation of RNNs did transformers solve?
options: Sequential processing — no parallelism, fading long-range memory
evolution-rnn-limitation.explain: RNNs read one token at a time and forget far-away context; attention sees the whole sequence at once, in parallel.

evolution-scaling-laws.prompt: What do LLM scaling laws describe?
options: How GPUs heat up under load
How loss falls predictably as model, data, and compute grow
evolution-scaling-laws.explain: Kaplan et al. and Chinchilla showed loss follows smooth power laws — making giant training runs a calculated bet.

learn-softmax-output.prompt: What does softmax turn logits into?
options: A probability distribution that sums to 1
learn-softmax-output.explain: exp() then normalize: every token gets a probability, and they all add up to exactly 1.

options: KV-cache memory, by sharing K/V heads across query heads
learn-gqa-saves.explain: Many query heads share a few K/V heads, shrinking the KV cache several-fold at long context.

options: Token positions, as rotations of the Q/K vectors
learn-rope-encodes.explain: Rotary position embeddings rotate query/key vectors by an angle proportional to position — relative order falls out of the dot product.

learn-moe-active.prompt: In a Mixture-of-Experts layer, which experts run for each token?
options: None — experts are only used in training
One fixed expert for the whole prompt
Only the top-k experts a router picks per token
learn-moe-active.explain: A router scores all experts per token and activates just the top-k, so compute stays small while capacity is huge.

learn-tokenizer-units.prompt: What units does a BPE tokenizer split text into?
options: Subword pieces from a learned vocabulary
learn-tokenizer-units.explain: Byte-pair encoding merges frequent character pairs into subwords — common words are one token, rare ones split.

calculator.title: LLM VRAM Calculator
calculator.subtitle: Will it run on your GPU? Estimate the memory any tracked open model needs — weights by quantization, KV cache by context — and see which GPUs can hold it.

verdict.maxContext: Fits with up to {ctx} tokens of context

notes.title: Assumptions
notes.moe-all-experts: Mixture-of-Experts: all {n} experts stay resident in memory — top-k routing reduces compute, not VRAM.
notes.mla-compressed: MLA caches a compressed latent (~576 values per token per layer) instead of full K/V heads.
notes.ssm-no-kv: State-space (Mamba) blocks keep a fixed-size state, so memory does not grow with context length.
notes.sliding-window: Sliding-window layers only cache the most recent {w} tokens.
notes.overhead-model: Runtime overhead is modeled as 0.75 GB + 5% for CUDA context, buffers and fragmentation.
notes.disclaimer: Estimates are ±10%. Real usage varies by runtime (llama.cpp, vLLM, MLX) and settings.

embed.title: Will it run on your GPU?
embed.atContext: estimated at {ctx} tokens of context, batch 1

items.q: How much VRAM does a 70B model need?
items.a: At Q4_K_M quantization the weights of a 70B model take about {w70q4}, and with KV cache and overhead the practical total at 8K context is around {t70q4} — so it fits a 48 GB card, or two 24 GB GPUs. At FP16 the weights alone are {w70fp16}.

items.q: What is quantization and how much memory does it save?
items.a: Quantization stores weights with fewer bits. FP16 uses 2 bytes per parameter; Q8_0 about 1.06; Q4_K_M about 0.61 — roughly a 3.3× saving versus FP16 with a small quality loss. An 8B model drops from {w8fp16} of weights to {w8q4}.

items.q: Why does a long context need more memory?
items.a: The KV cache stores keys and values for every past token, so it grows linearly with context length and batch size. For an 8B-class model (32 layers, 8 KV heads) each 8K tokens of context adds about {kv8at8k} on top of the weights.

items.q: Can I split a model across multiple GPUs?
items.a: Yes — tensor or pipeline parallelism in runtimes like vLLM spreads the weights and KV cache across cards, and frontier-size open models (400B+) require it. The calculator shows how many GPUs of the selected type the total would need.

footer.dataNote: Architecture data is read directly from each model's config.json and safetensors index — no weights downloaded.

items.a: llm-xray is a free, interactive site that ranks the latest open-source large language models by downloads and benchmarks, then lets you x-ray any model’s architecture — attention, normalization, MLP, and Mixture-of-Experts — layer by layer in 2D and 3D, so you can see how LLMs actually work.

It tracks open-weight LLMs from official Hugging Face accounts (Llama, Qwen, Mistral, DeepSeek, Gemma, Phi and more), refreshed automatically. Each model page is built from the real config and safetensors index — layer count, hidden size, attention type (MHA/GQA/MQA/MLA), context length, and MoE setup.

Models are sorted by Hugging Face downloads and likes, with benchmark columns such as MMLU, and you can filter by family, modality, attention type, and Mixture-of-Experts. The compare page puts models side by side across 12+ metrics.

items.q: What is the difference between MHA, GQA, MQA, and MLA?
items.a: They are attention variants that trade quality for a smaller key/value cache. MHA gives every head its own keys and values; GQA shares them across groups of heads; MQA shares one set across all heads; MLA compresses them into a latent. Fewer KV heads mean faster, cheaper inference.

A Mixture-of-Experts model replaces the dense feed-forward layer with many expert networks and a router that activates only the top-k experts per token (for example top-2 of 8 in Mixtral). This makes the model much larger in total parameters while keeping the compute per token low.

items.q: Is llm-xray free, and do I need an account?
items.a: Yes, it is completely free and needs no account. It is an educational product for students, engineers, and anyone learning how transformers and LLMs work, available in English, Russian, and Uzbek.

realRun.title: Run a real model
realRun.subtitle: The demos above are illustrative. This runs a real small model — DistilGPT-2 — entirely in your browser. Click Run to load it once (a few MB) and see the actual next-token probabilities.
realRun.unsupported: Your browser can’t run in-browser models (module Web Workers unavailable).
realRun.note: Real output from DistilGPT-2 via transformers.js, computed on your device — nothing is sent to a server.
